goal: Design one-afternoon TA onboarding: launch → template → inspect → modify → save, each with fallback.
thoughts: TA will grade, not research — keep to supported 5.x workflow. Pedagogic spine: 5 steps, each has a 'if this fails, try...' branch. Gazebo→Isaac mental model: USD composition is the biggest shift, not the physics. Do NOT drop into Python on day one; Python step is 'paste this snippet', not 'write code'.
code:
# Session plan (TA follows top-to-bottom):

# (a) Launch Kit — if splash crash, check driver first
scene_aware_starter_prompts()          # shows TA what is safe to try

# (b) Load a friendly starter — tabletop manipulation, small stage
list_scene_templates()
load_scene_template(template_name="<TABLETOP_MANIPULATION_STARTER>")
# Fallback: if load fails, try <WAREHOUSE_STARTER>

# (c) Inspect — teach stage panel via run_stage_analysis
run_stage_analysis()
# Fallback: scene_summary() if analysis output too long

# (d) Modify ONE thing — add a cube, no scripting
add_reference(prim_path="/World/TestCube", reference_path="<CUBE_USD>")
# Fallback: set_attribute on an existing prim if add_reference fails

# (e) Save
save_stage(stage_path="<TA_FIRST_SCENE_USD>")

# Gazebo→Isaac note: USD composition (refs + variants) is the mind-shift, not physics.
failure_modes: Dropping TA into Python on day one — overwhelm
Skipping save step — TA loses afternoon's work
Picking a template that needs custom extension enabled — unsupported fringe
Onboarding doc references UI menu that moved in 5.1
